title: Function Calling
lesson:
On its own, a language model can only do one thing: produce text. It cannot check the weather, run a database query, or send an email. It has no hands. Function calling is how we give it hands — not by letting it run code (it never does), but by letting it ask, in a strict, machine-readable format, for a function in your program to be run. Your code runs the function, hands the result back, and the model carries on. Function calling is the original, lower-level name for this mechanism; the broader product-level idea built on top of it is Tool Calling. Here we zoom in on the actual nuts and bolts.

The waiter and the kitchen

Think of the model as a waiter. The waiter takes your order in plain English — "I'd like the salmon" — but the waiter never cooks. They write a ticket in the kitchen's exact format (table 4, salmon, no capers) and pin it up. The kitchen (your code) cooks and passes a plate back. The waiter then brings it to you and explains it. Function calling is the model writing that ticket. The crucial part: the ticket has a fixed shape the kitchen understands. A scribbled "fish maybe?" gets the order thrown back. So before any conversation, you hand the model a menu of functions it's allowed to order from, each with a precise schema (a written description of the function's name, what it does, and the exact arguments it takes).

The four-step dance

Almost every function-calling system follows the same loop:

  1. Declare: you send the model the user's message plus the list of available functions and their schemas.
  2. Decide: instead of plain text, the model may reply with a structured request — "call get_weather with {city: 'Paris'}". This is just text the model generated; nothing has run yet.
  3. Execute: your code reads that request, actually runs get_weather('Paris'), and gets back, say, 18°C, rain.
  4. Return: you feed that result back into the model, which now writes a normal human reply: "It's 18°C and rainy in Paris — bring a coat."

The single most important thing to grasp: the model only proposes the call. You always execute it. The model is a very good order-taker, not a chef.

The schema is everything

How does the model know which arguments to send? You tell it, in a structured description usually written as a JSON-style schema (JSON is a plain-text format of labelled key-value pairs that both humans and code can read). This is where most of your effort goes — clear names and descriptions act like a prompt for the model.

{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get the current weather for a city.",
  "parameters": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "city": { "type": "string", "description": "City name, e.g. Paris" },
      "units": { "type": "string", "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"] }
    },
    "required": ["city"]
  }
}

That enum (a fixed list of allowed values) stops the model inventing units: 'kelvin'. The model reads these descriptions like instructions, so vague ones ("the input") produce vague calls. Treat schema-writing as part of system-prompt design.

A second analogy: filling in a form

Picture a paper form at the bank with labelled boxes: NAME, AMOUNT, DATE. You can scrawl anything in the margins, but the teller only reads the boxes. The schema is that form. Function calling forces the model to stop free-writing and fill in the boxes — which is exactly why it's so reliable. The output isn't a paragraph you have to parse with fragile pattern-matching; it's clean, typed data your code can use directly. In fact, this is why the same machinery is reused for plain structured output — sometimes you define a "function" you never even run, just to force the model to answer in a strict shape (e.g. extract {name, email} from a messy email).

# The loop your code runs
msg = model.chat(user_text, functions=[weather_schema])
if msg.function_call:                       # model proposed a call
    args = json.loads(msg.function_call.arguments)
    result = get_weather(**args)            # YOU run the real function
    final = model.chat(user_text, function_result=result)
    print(final.text)

Why this unlocks real products

Function calling is the bridge between a chatbot and software that does things. A travel assistant calls search_flights; a support bot calls refund_order; a coding helper calls run_tests. Chain several of these calls together and let the model decide the order, and you've built the core of an AI Agent running an Agentic Workflow. But it all rests on this humble loop: declare, decide, execute, return. Master the loop, and everything fancier is just more functions on the menu.
mentalModel: The model is a waiter, not a chef. It writes a precise kitchen ticket (the function call in your schema's exact shape) but never cooks — your code runs the real function and hands the plate back for the model to serve.
mistakes: Thinking the model runs the function. It never does. It only emits a request in your schema's format; your code must execute it and feed the result back. Forgetting the 'return' step leaves the model talking to itself with no answer.
Writing vague schemas. The model reads your name and description fields like instructions. 'the data' or 'input1' gets you garbage arguments. Treat each description as careful prompt engineering.

Trusting the arguments blindly. The model can hallucinate values, miss required fields, or output slightly malformed JSON. Always validate and sanitise arguments before running anything — especially calls that delete, pay, or email.

Offering 30 functions at once. A bloated menu confuses the model into picking the wrong one and bloats every prompt. Keep the function list short and relevant; fetch a smaller set per task if you have many.
Skipping required and enum constraints. Without them the model invents optional fields or out-of-range values (like an unsupported currency). Tight schemas are guardrails, not red tape.

Confusing function calling with tool calling. They're the same machinery; 'function calling' is the original low-level term for the request format, while 'tool calling' is the broader, product-level wrapper. Don't expect two different mechanisms.
exercise:
Pick one simple function you could write, e.g. convert_currency(amount, from_ccy, to_ccy). On paper (or in code), write its JSON schema: a clear name, a one-line description, each parameter with a type and description, an enum of allowed currencies, and a required list. Now write out, by hand, what the model would emit for the message 'How much is 50 dollars in euros?' — then what your code does with it, and the final human reply. Trace all four steps: declare, decide, execute, return.

Stretch goal: Add a second function (get_exchange_rate) and write the message 'Convert 50 USD to the currency with the strongest rate against the dollar today.' Show how the model would need to call two functions in sequence — and where your code feeds the first result back so the model can decide the second call.
solution:
For the single function, the model should emit a call to convert_currency with {amount: 50, from_ccy: 'USD', to_ccy: 'EUR'}. Note it correctly maps 'dollars' to the enum value 'USD'; that mapping is exactly what a clear description buys you. Your code runs the real conversion (say it returns 46.2), you feed 46.2 back, and only then does the model write 'About €46.20'. The key insight learners miss: the model produced no euros itself — it just filled the form, your code did the maths, and the model phrased the answer.

For the stretch goal, the model can't answer in one shot because it doesn't know today's rates. It first calls get_exchange_rate, your code returns live numbers, you feed those back, and the model then decides which currency is strongest and issues the convert_currency call. That back-and-forth — call, return, call again — is the seed of an agent: the same loop, just repeated until the job's done.
summary: Function calling lets a text-only model request that one of your functions be run, in a strict machine-readable shape it cannot easily get wrong. You declare a menu of functions with clear schemas; the model decides which to call and with what arguments; your code executes the real function and returns the result; the model then writes a human reply. The model proposes, your code disposes — it never runs anything itself. Clear schemas (names, descriptions, enum, required) are most of the work, and chaining these calls is the foundation of AI agents.

keyTerms.def: A mechanism where the model outputs a structured request to run one of your functions, with specific arguments, instead of plain text. Your code runs it and returns the result.
A written description of a function — its name, what it does, and the exact arguments it accepts — that tells the model how to call it correctly.
The specific values the model fills in for a function's inputs, e.g. {city: 'Paris'}. The model proposes them; your code uses them.
The model only generates a call request as text. Actually running the function ('execution') is done by your code, never by the model.
A schema rule listing the only allowed values for a field, so the model can't invent an out-of-range option like an unsupported currency.
A schema rule naming which arguments must always be present, so the model doesn't omit a value the function needs to run.
Using the same function-calling machinery purely to force a strict response shape (like extracting {name, email}), sometimes without ever running a real function.
The four-step cycle behind every function call: declare available functions, model decides a call, your code executes it, you return the result for the model's final reply.
